stories.headline: 18-year-old arrested on suspicion of murder in Thornton house party shooting
stories.summary:
Thornton police arrested 18-year-old Cesar Licon Jr. on suspicion of murder following a deadly shooting at a house party early Sunday morning. The shooting occurred inside a home in the 3600 block of East 91st Avenue at approximately 1:45 a.m., killing a 44-year-old man who was transported to a hospital and later died.

Licon was identified as a suspect and arrested Sunday night. He is being held without bail at the Adams County Jail, and his next court date was not immediately available.

The victim has not yet been identified publicly; the Adams County Coroner's Office is expected to release that information. Thornton police have not disclosed how detectives linked Licon to the shooting.

stories.headline: Suspected drunk driver leaves 21-year-old Denver woman with severe injuries after downtown crosswalk crash
stories.summary:
A 21-year-old Denver woman suffered severe injuries after a suspected drunk driver struck her in a downtown crosswalk, according to authorities. The crash occurred in the downtown Denver area and has prompted a criminal investigation.

The incident adds to a recent string of serious traffic crashes involving vulnerable road users in the Denver metro area. Authorities have not released the name of the suspect or additional details about charges filed.

stories.headline: Independent candidate launches late campaign against Democratic Socialist Melat Kiros for Colorado's 1st Congressional District
stories.summary:
Dr. Shimon Blau, a Denver pain management physician, is petitioning to appear on the November ballot as an independent candidate in Colorado's 1st Congressional District following Melat Kiros' upset primary victory over 15-term Congresswoman Diana DeGette. Blau filed paperwork with the Colorado Secretary of State's office Monday and must gather 1,500 valid signatures by Thursday at 5 p.m. to qualify.

Blau said his candidacy is driven in part by concerns about comments Kiros made regarding Israel and the Gaza war, as well as her identification as a Democratic Socialist. He described his own politics as moderate, leaning left on social issues and right on fiscal ones, and said he has attracted support from Denver's Jewish community as well as Christian churches in the city.

If Blau qualifies, he would face Kiros and Republican candidate Christy Peterson in the November general election. Kiros' primary victory drew national media attention from outlets including The New York Times, Wall Street Journal, and Washington Post, with analysts drawing comparisons to recent Democratic Socialist electoral successes in New York and Seattle.

stories.headline: Metro Denver housing market holds steady, with median sales price near $600,000 for fourth consecutive year
stories.summary:
The median price of a home sold in the Denver metro area during the first half of 2026 was $599,950, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' June Market Trends Report — nearly identical to the $600,000 median recorded in the first halves of 2022 and 2025. Single-family homes posted a June median of $675,000, while condos and townhomes came in at $391,750.

Closings fell 6.6% between May and June to 3,924 and are down 5.6% year-over-year, though the first-half total of roughly 21,000 to 22,000 sales has remained consistent over four years. Mid-year inventory stood at 12,744 listings as of June 30, down from 14,007 a year earlier but more than double inventory levels seen in 2022 and 2023.

Condo prices have declined about 5% over four years, a trend attributed to rising homeowners association fees driven by higher insurance and maintenance costs. DMAR Market Trends Committee chair Amanda Snitker said mortgage rates remain the key variable, and absent a significant change, the market is expected to continue its stable trajectory through the rest of 2026.

stories.headline: EchoStar CEO exits after Englewood-based company's Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing
stories.summary:
The top executive at EchoStar, the Englewood-based satellite television and telecom company founded by Charlie Ergen, has resigned following the firm's Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing. The departure was attributed to what the company described as 'a change in strategic direction.'

EchoStar, which operates the Dish Network brand, has faced significant financial headwinds in recent years as satellite pay-TV subscribers have declined and the company accumulated substantial debt. The leadership change signals a potential restructuring of operations as the company navigates bankruptcy proceedings.

The executive's exit adds to uncertainty for EchoStar employees and business partners in the Denver metro area, where the company has long maintained a major presence and employed thousands of workers.

stories.headline: In-N-Out Burger proposes new location in Denver's Green Valley Ranch neighborhood
stories.summary:
In-N-Out Burger has submitted plans to the City of Denver for a new restaurant at 17600 E. 56th Ave. in the Green Valley Ranch neighborhood, near the intersection of E. 56th Avenue and Telluride Street. The proposed 3,890-square-foot building would feature a drive-thru, 84 indoor seats, a covered outdoor patio with 32 seats, bicycle parking, and an on-site parking lot.

Matt Loevenguth, director of real estate at In-N-Out Burger, confirmed the application in a statement to The Denver Post but cautioned that the project is still in the development approval process. The California-based chain, which made its Colorado debut in late 2020, now operates more than 10 locations across the state.

The proposed Green Valley Ranch site would add to In-N-Out's Denver-area footprint, which currently includes a location in Aurora. The chain's Aurora opening in 2020 drew massive crowds, with traffic backing up for miles and customers waiting up to 14 hours.

stories.headline: Nuggets face pivotal decision on Peyton Watson as $177 million luxury tax bill looms
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets are reportedly weighing whether to match any outside offer sheet for forward Peyton Watson, with reports suggesting ownership may be reluctant to do so given the franchise's historic luxury tax obligations. The Nuggets are facing a projected luxury tax bill of approximately $177 million, raising questions about their willingness to retain all of their young roster pieces.

Additionally, guard Jalen Pickett has departed the organization, with Denver now exploring options to back up Jamal Murray at point guard. Tyus Jones, who moved ahead of Pickett in the rotation last season, is an unrestricted free agent and a potential target.

The roster decisions come as the Nuggets navigate a complex salary cap situation while trying to maintain a contending roster around two-time MVP Nikola Jokic, who has separately indicated he is focused on his contract situation.

stories.headline: Broncos training camp approaches with Bo Nix building momentum and all draft picks signed
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are preparing for the start of training camp with quarterback Bo Nix entering his second season under center following a promising debut year. All of the team's 2026 draft picks have signed their contracts, clearing a logistical hurdle ahead of camp.

Nix has drawn praise from former Broncos quarterback Jake Plummer, who told the Denver Gazette that Nix's natural instincts are a key asset that cannot be coached. The Broncos are counting on Nix to continue his development as the franchise cornerstone under head coach Sean Payton.

Denver's offseason has also focused on defensive continuity, with the team looking to replace departed defensive lineman John Franklin-Myers and relying on second-year player Sai'vion Jones to take on a larger role in 2026.

stories.headline: Rockies face uncertainty heading into MLB trade deadline as front office signals flexible approach
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies are entering the August 3 MLB trade deadline with no clear direction, according to the Denver Gazette, which described the team's posture as having 'Forrest Gump chocolate-box vibes' — nobody quite knows what they are going to get. President of baseball operations Paul DePodesta has not publicly signaled whether the club intends to be a buyer, seller, or stand-pat at the deadline.

The Rockies have struggled through another difficult season and face decisions on several veterans whose contracts or roles could make them trade candidates. The deadline hits at 4 p.m. on Monday, Aug. 3.

Colorado's front office has been engaged in a broader long-term rebuild since the departure of Nolan Arenado in 2021, and the trade deadline represents another opportunity — or missed opportunity — to accelerate or redirect that process.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres fire reaches 94,000 acres with containment growing to 16% as thousands remain displaced
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire in Pueblo and Custer counties grew to nearly 94,000 acres as of Tuesday, remaining the seventh-largest wildfire in Colorado history. Containment improved from 12% to 16% on Tuesday, though fire officials warned that conditions remain dynamic and active.

Thousands of residents continue to be displaced from their homes since the fire began June 29, and evacuation orders remain in effect across multiple southern Colorado communities. The blaze has destroyed at least 180 structures.

A separate, small lightning-sparked grass fire broke out Tuesday evening at Cheyenne Mountain State Park near Colorado Springs, but crews from the Colorado Springs Fire Department and Cheyenne Mountain Space Force Station quickly contained it at approximately one-tenth of an acre. The minor fire required no evacuations.

stories.headline: Cyclist killed after being struck by semitrailer in Fort Collins construction zone
stories.summary:
A woman was killed Monday evening after a semitrailer struck her as she crossed East Mulberry Street at Whedbee Street in Fort Collins, according to Fort Collins police. Officers responded to a 911 call at approximately 6:20 p.m. and found the woman at the scene; she was pronounced dead on arrival.

The semitrailer was associated with an ongoing construction project along a stretch of Mulberry Street. Investigators believe the woman was crossing from the north side of the road to the south when she was struck. Construction personnel were reportedly present at the time.

The truck driver remained at the scene following the crash. The Fort Collins Police Services Collision Reconstruction and Scene Handling team has taken over the investigation. The woman's identity has not yet been released. Anyone with information is asked to contact Officer Matt Brede at (970) 221-6842.

stories.headline: Denver food scene heats up in July with pop-ups, grilling championships, and new restaurant concepts
stories.summary:
Denver's restaurant and food event calendar is packed for July, with a variety of pop-ups, chef collaborations, and specialty events running through the month. Highlights include a summer-long series of Friday wine pairings at Bon Vin in West Highland featuring chefs from prominent Denver restaurants, and a new pop-up concept called Tequilería Alteño from Michelin-starred chef Johnny Curiel operating at the Clayton Hotel through the summer.

King Arthur Baking's pop-up location at 2800 Walnut St. has launched Cottage Bakery Sundays, showcasing local cottage bakers each Sunday in July. The Up NXT Denver rotating culinary incubator at Bear Leek on Walnut Street continues its Sunday series with Magnolion, a Southern-Asian crossover concept from chefs Cody Peeler and Tiffany Leong.

The events reflect continued vitality in Denver's dining scene, offering residents a range of price points and cuisines throughout the summer months.

joke.joke: I can't be spontaneous on purpose. If I plan to be spontaneous, it's no longer spontaneous. It's scheduled whimsy.

topStory.headline: Trump Declares Ceasefire 'Over' as U.S. and Iran Trade Strikes Over Strait of Hormuz
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump declared Wednesday that the ceasefire and interim agreement between the United States and Iran are "over" after the two countries exchanged military strikes centered on the Strait of Hormuz. The escalation began when Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the strategic waterway, prompting U.S. Central Command to strike more than 80 Iranian targets, including air defense systems, command and control networks, coastal radar sites, anti-ship missile capabilities, and more than 60 small boats belonging to Iran's Revolutionary Guard. U.S. officials described the strikes, carried out by Air Force jets and Navy tactical aircraft, as larger than previous American retaliatory actions against Iran.

Iran responded by launching ballistic missiles and drones at U.S. Gulf allies Bahrain and Kuwait. Kuwait's military confirmed it intercepted two ballistic missiles and 13 drones that breached its airspace, with authorities reporting no casualties. Iran did not formally claim responsibility for the earlier attacks on commercial ships, though Iranian state media said at least one vessel had ignored warnings from Iranian forces.

Trump, speaking at a NATO summit in Turkey, said negotiations with Iran were "just a waste of time" while allowing diplomats to continue talks. Iran's Foreign Ministry said U.S. and Israeli actions had "rendered key and fundamental elements of the agreement to end the war ineffective," though Tehran had not formally declared the ceasefire finished as of Wednesday morning. Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf wrote on social media that "the era of bullying and extortion is over."

At the heart of the dispute is control over the Strait of Hormuz, through which a significant share of global oil and commercial cargo transits. Under a June 17 memorandum of understanding, Iran agreed to use its "best efforts" to ensure safe passage of commercial vessels for 60 days at no charge, but Iran has since demanded ships coordinate with Iranian authorities and declared IMO-designated routes unacceptable. The International Maritime Organization's secretary-general, Arsenio Dominguez, condemned the attacks, noting that several hundred ships carrying approximately 6,000 seafarers remain stranded in the Persian Gulf with no exit route other than the Strait of Hormuz.

The renewed hostilities come as Iran holds a multi-day state funeral for Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in the U.S.-Israeli strikes that began the conflict in February. Negotiations between Washington and Tehran had been paused pending the conclusion of funeral proceedings on Thursday, but the fresh exchange of strikes has deepened uncertainty over whether any peace process can be revived. Trump separately warned that Iran had placed him at the top of its targeting list and said the country must not be permitted to acquire a nuclear weapon.
